name: The Legend of the Porma and Curueño Rivers - circular from Lillo
name_original: La Leyenda de los ríos Porma y Curueño – circular desde Lillo
region_intro: Touring cycling around Puebla De Lillo offers routes through the Cantabrian Mountains, characterized by mountainous terrain, green valleys, and significant forest areas. The region features natural glacial lakes and rivers such as the Porma River, providing varied scenery for cyclists. Mountain passes like Puerto de La Tarna and Puerto de las Señales present demanding climbs with consistent gradients. This area is situated within the Montaña de Riaño y Mampodre Regional Park, offering diverse landscapes for outdoor activities. Best touring cycling routes around Puebla De Lillo The most popular touring cycling route is Porma Reservoir Loop, a 20.3 miles (32.7 km) trail that takes 2 hours 30 minutes to complete. This difficult route circles the Porma Reservoir, offering views of the water and surrounding hills. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Puebla de Lillo: Tarna and Las Señales Pass Loop, a difficult 32.9 miles (53.0 km) path. This route traverses two notable mountain passes, providing significant elevation gain and panoramic views of the Cantabrian Mountains. Local touring cyclists also love the The Legend of the Porma and Curueño Rivers - circular from Lillo, a 42.5 miles (68.4 km) trail leading through river valleys and varied terrain, often completed in about 4 hours 34 minutes. Touring cycling around Puebla De Lillo is defined by mountain passes, river valleys, and dense pine and beech forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, with a focus on challenging routes. The routes in Puebla De Lillo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from around 10 reviews. More than 150 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Puebla De Lillo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when touring cycling around Puebla de Lillo?
A: Touring cycling around Puebla de Lillo primarily features mountainous terrain within the Cantabrian Mountains. You'll encounter green valleys, dramatic peaks, and significant forest areas, including native Scots pine, beech, and birch forests. The region is also characterized by natural glacial lakes and rivers like the Porma, offering diverse and scenic landscapes.

Q: Are there many challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists in Puebla de Lillo?
A: Yes, Puebla de Lillo is well-suited for experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge. The area is known for its demanding climbs, particularly over mountain passes like Puerto de La Tarna and Puerto de las Señales, which offer consistent gradients and panoramic views. Out of 21 available routes, 18 are classified as difficult, providing ample opportunities for strenuous rides.

Q: Can I find circular touring cycling routes in the Puebla de Lillo area?
A: Yes, there are several excellent circular touring cycling routes available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1032744796/porma-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Porma Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a 32.7 km ride circling the reservoir, while &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037009721/the-legend-of-the-porma-and-curueno-rivers-circular-from-lillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Legend of the Porma and Curueño Rivers - circular from Lillo&lt;/a&gt; is a longer 68.4 km option through river valleys.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along the touring cycling routes in Puebla de Lillo?
A: The routes around Puebla de Lillo offer stunning natural attractions. You can cycle past beautiful glacial lakes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5554953" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Isoba Lake&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5543140" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Porma Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. The region also boasts picturesque waterfalls like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3904335" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tabayón del Mongayu Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5554961" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Forfogones Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, often found amidst dense forests and river valleys.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Puebla de Lillo?
A: The touring cycling routes in Puebla de Lillo are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.6 stars from numerous reviews. Cyclists often praise the challenging nature of the routes, the breathtaking panoramic views from mountain passes, and the diverse natural beauty, including lakes, rivers, and forests.

Q: Are there any routes that feature significant mountain passes?
A: Absolutely. The region is famous for its mountain passes. A notable route is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037022627/puebla-de-lillo-tarna-and-las-senales-pass-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puebla de Lillo: Tarna and Las Señales Pass Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which traverses both the Puerto de La Tarna and Puerto de las Señales, offering significant elevation gain and expansive views of the Cantabrian Mountains.

Q: What kind of forests can I expect to see while cycling in Puebla de Lillo?
A: Puebla de Lillo is home to significant forest areas, including the unique Pinar de Lillo, one of the few native Scots pine forests on the Iberian Peninsula. You'll also find beautiful beech and birch forests, which are particularly vibrant in autumn, adding rich colors to your cycling experience.

Q: Are there any routes that start from Maraña and explore the surrounding area?
A: Yes, if you're starting from Maraña, you can explore routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40011407" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Maraña Hostel – Riaño Reservoir loop from Maraña&lt;/a&gt;. This route offers a challenging 59.9 km ride with views of the Riaño Reservoir and surrounding landscapes.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Puebla de Lillo?
A: While the guide doesn't specify the absolute best time, the region's mountainous terrain and forests suggest that spring and autumn would offer pleasant temperatures and stunning scenery, especially with the vibrant colors of the beech and birch forests in autumn. Summer can also be good, but be prepared for potentially warmer temperatures on climbs.

Q: Are there any cultural landmarks or points of interest near the cycling routes?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, Puebla de Lillo offers cultural points of interest. The medieval Torreón, now serving as a cultural and interpretation center for the Picos de Europa, provides insight into the region's heritage and can be a fascinating stop during your cycling adventures.

Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in total around Puebla de Lillo?
A: There are over 20 touring cycling routes available around Puebla de Lillo, offering a wide range of options for exploring the diverse landscapes of the Montaña de Riaño y Mampodre Regional Park.
tips: Although this reservoir was officially renamed Juan Benet in 1994, after the death of the engineer in charge of its construction, it is still popularly known as the Porma reservoir or swamp, due to the river that supplies it, or even Vegamián, after one of the towns that were flooded after its construction.

This viewpoint not only offers views of the swamp, which after all can be observed from different points, but also has the Peña Utrero in the background, offering one more picture, even a postcard. La Peña Utrero receives its name from the town of Utrero, which was also partially destroyed with the construction of the reservoir.
This section crosses an incredible stone canyon on a national highway. Virtually no cars and some scary views.
This climb can be a bit demanding but the change of valley will give you a new perspective on the route.
The La Respina talc mine is open pit and has dimensions close to 600 meters long, 200 meters wide and about 100 meters deep. It is currently partially flooded. Work on it stopped in 1992.

At a confluence of rivers and streams in the Porma valley, is this serene town that serves as a stop for athletes of multiple disciplines, both in summer and winter. Close to a beautiful thousand-year-old pine forest of the same name (although also known as the Villaoscura pine forest) and framed within the Montaña de Riaño y Mampodre Regional Park, you can imagine how beautiful the surroundings of Puebla de Lillo are. As beautiful as beautiful is the story of Polma and Curienno, which gives its name to this valley and the neighboring valley. In different versions of the legend, Polma is killed by her lover after being released from her wedding, or she is the one who fights disguised as a warrior to avenge the death of her lover.

waypoints.name: View of Embalse del Porma and Peña Utrero
